terms.name: Product-Market Fit (PMF)
terms.description: The degree to which a product satisfies strong market demand, measured by customer retention, satisfaction, and willingness to pay premium pricing. Occurs when a product delivers significant value to a clearly defined market segment.
terms.usageExample: BAIV AIR product achieved PMF with Ellis Score of 45% and retention rate of 42% among marketing agency customers.
terms.usageContext: Used to assess whether a product has successfully found its market and should scale
terms.businessMeaning: Validation that customers want and will pay for your product; signals readiness for scaling
terms.technicalMeaning: Measured state where quantitative metrics (Ellis Score >=w%, Retention >=x%) exceed defined thresholds
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents use PMF score to recommend go-to-market strategies and investment decisions

terms.name: Organization Context
terms.description: Polymorphic organization entity supporting multiple organizational roles in PMF process including platform owner, target customer, actual client, competitor, supply chain partner, and market analog.
terms.usageExample: BAIV platform (platformOwner) conducts PMF discovery with Widget Corp (targetCustomer) while analyzing Competitor X (competitor).
terms.usageContext: Used throughout PMF process to identify roles and relationships between organizations
terms.businessMeaning: Defines who is involved in the PMF process and their relationship to each other
terms.technicalMeaning: Entity with orgId foreign key to org:Organization, with type classification and role assignment
terms.constraints: MUST have valid orgId; MUST have at least one organization type; Platform owner required for process instantiation
terms.relationships: Links to org:Organization via orgId; contains pmf:PMFProcess instances
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents use org context to personalize recommendations and segment analysis by organization type

terms.name: Platform Owner
terms.description: Organization owning and operating the PMF platform. Examples include BAIV, AIR, W4M. Acts as the primary executor of PMF processes.
terms.usageExample: BAIV acts as platform owner when conducting PMF analysis for client products.
terms.usageContext: Identifies the organization implementing and managing the PMF framework
terms.businessMeaning: The company or entity responsible for executing PMF methodology
terms.technicalMeaning: Organization type enum value; cardinality 1..1 per PMF process instance
terms.constraints: MUST be identified before PMF process instantiation (Rule OR-001)
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents use platform owner context to apply organization-specific configurations and branding

terms.name: Target Customer
terms.description: Prospective customer organization in PMF discovery phase. Subject of customer discovery and problem validation research.
terms.usageExample: Widget Corp identified as target customer during discovery phase; transitioned to actual client after purchase.
terms.usageContext: Used during Customer Discovery and Problem Validation phases
terms.businessMeaning: Organizations being researched as potential customers
terms.technicalMeaning: Organization type enum value; cardinality 1..* per PMF process (minimum n required)
terms.constraints: MUST have valid orgId from org:Organization (Rule OR-002); Minimum n organizations required (Rule OR-003)
terms.relationships: May transition to actual client status; subject of interview data
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents prioritize target customers by fit score and engagement level

terms.name: Actual Client
terms.description: Active customer organization using product/service. Participant in PMF validation and optimization.
terms.usageExample: Widget Corp became actual client after MVP purchase; contributes retention and NPS data.
terms.usageContext: Used in PMF Testing, Optimization, and Maintenance phases
terms.businessMeaning: Paying customers providing validation data
terms.technicalMeaning: Organization type enum value; source of retention, NPS, and usage metrics
terms.relationships: Evolved from target customer; provides PMF validation data
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents analyze actual client behavior to predict PMF metrics and churn risk

terms.name: Competitor
terms.description: Competing organization in target market. Used for competitive analysis and differentiation reference.
terms.usageExample: Competitor X analyzed as alternative solution; PMF requires 3X-5X improvement over their offering.
terms.usageContext: Used throughout PMF process for competitive benchmarking
terms.businessMeaning: Organizations offering alternative solutions in target market
terms.technicalMeaning: Organization type enum value; benchmark for improvement calculations
terms.constraints: Minimum n competitors required for valid analysis (Rule OR-003)
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents track competitor features and positioning to suggest differentiation strategies

terms.name: Organization Bridge Entity
terms.description: Bridging entity connecting OAA Organization Ontology to PMF Process while preserving referential integrity. Maps org:Organization to pmf:OrganizationContext.
terms.usageExample: Bridge entity links Widget Corp (orgId: org-12345) to PMF context (pmfContextId: pmf-ctx-789).
terms.usageContext: Created when organization is added to PMF process
terms.businessMeaning: Ensures organizations in PMF process are properly registered in master organization registry
terms.technicalMeaning: Foreign key relationship with metadata; enforces referential integrity constraint
terms.constraints: sourceOrgId MUST exist in org:Organization; pmfContextId MUST be unique within PMF instance
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents use bridge metadata to determine data flow permissions and relationship strength

terms.name: Strategy Bridge Entity
terms.description: Connects organizational strategy/VSOM to PMF value proposition and product. Links the chain: org:Strategy → vsom:VSOM → pmf:ValueProposition → pmf:Product.
terms.usageExample: Strategy bridge connects BAIV Growth Strategy to AIR Value Proposition: 'AI-powered marketing visibility 10x faster'.
terms.usageContext: Ensures products align with organizational strategic objectives
terms.businessMeaning: Maintains traceability from strategic vision to product implementation
terms.technicalMeaning: Foreign key relationships enforcing strategic alignment constraint
terms.constraints: strategyId MUST exist in org:Strategy; vsomId MUST exist in vsom:VSOM (Rule SR-001)
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents validate strategic alignment and suggest course corrections when products drift from strategy

terms.name: Product Bridge Entity
terms.description: Connects value proposition to product implementation and PMF assessment. Links: pmf:ValueProposition → pmf:Product → pmf:PMFProcess.
terms.usageExample: Product bridge connects AIR Value Proposition to AIR Product v2.1 and its PMF assessment cycle.
terms.usageContext: Created when product enters PMF process
terms.businessMeaning: Ensures products being assessed for PMF have clear value propositions
terms.technicalMeaning: Foreign key relationships with one-to-many cardinality (product can have multiple PMF cycles)
terms.constraints: valuePropositionId MUST exist before product instantiation; productId unique within org scope
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents track PMF progression across multiple assessment cycles for trend analysis

terms.name: Three-Layer Market Winner Framework
terms.description: Heuristic framework stating market winners execute on three layers: (1) Business - CX and monetization, (2) Solution - product solving problems with minimal friction, (3) Technology - core infrastructure.
terms.usageExample: BAIV evaluates AIR product across three layers: (1) freemium monetization model, (2) AI-powered content optimization, (3) cloud-native ML infrastructure.
terms.usageContext: Applied during Customer Discovery to assess market positioning
terms.businessMeaning: Framework for understanding comprehensive requirements to win in market
terms.technicalMeaning: Evaluation framework with three mandatory assessment dimensions
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents score products across three layers and identify weakest areas needing improvement

terms.name: 3X-5X Improvement Threshold
terms.description: Heuristic requirement that new solution must be 3-5 times better than current alternatives to justify customer switching costs, time, and effort.
terms.usageExample: AIR product must deliver 3-5x faster content optimization than manual methods to overcome learning curve and switching costs.
terms.usageContext: Applied during Problem Validation and Solution Development
terms.businessMeaning: Products must deliver dramatically superior value to convince customers to switch from existing solutions
terms.technicalMeaning: improvementMultiplier >= 3.0 in quantitative metrics (time saved, cost reduced, quality improved)
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents calculate improvement multiplier from customer feedback and flag products not meeting 3X threshold

terms.name: Sharpest Problems First Prioritization
terms.description: Prioritization framework focusing roadmap on solving biggest, most beneficial problems first as these have highest correlation to PMF success. Uses 2x2 matrix: Difficulty (Easy/Hard) vs Problem Severity (Low/Sharp).
terms.usageExample: AIR team prioritizes 'content optimization speed' (Easy to solve + Sharp problem) over 'multi-language support' (Hard to solve + Low impact).
terms.usageContext: Applied during Customer Discovery and Solution Development
terms.businessMeaning: Focus limited resources on problems with highest customer impact and business value
terms.technicalMeaning: 2x2 prioritization matrix with quadrants: Quick Wins, Major Projects, Fill-ins, Question Marks
terms.constraints: Sharpest problems prioritization MUST be completed before MVP development (Rule PR-002)
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents score problems on severity and difficulty dimensions, auto-generate priority rankings

terms.name: Ellis PMF Score
terms.description: Percentage of customers who would be 'very disappointed' if they could no longer use the product. Invented by Sean Ellis as key PMF indicator. Threshold: >=w% (typically 40%).
terms.usageExample: AIR product Ellis Score: 45% of users would be very disappointed without it, exceeding 40% PMF threshold.
terms.usageContext: Measured during PMF Testing phase via customer survey
terms.businessMeaning: Indicates strength of customer dependency and product stickiness
terms.technicalMeaning: Decimal value 0.00-1.00; >= w threshold required for PMF achievement (Rule PR-004)
terms.constraints: ellisScore MUST be decimal between 0.00 and 1.00 (Rule VC-001)
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents track Ellis Score trends and alert when dropping below threshold

terms.name: Willingness to Pay (WTP) Analysis
terms.description: Research methodology determining price customers are willing to pay for solution. Follows 9 Rules of Pricing framework from 'Monetizing Innovation' by Madhavan Ramanujam and Georg Tacke.
terms.usageExample: WTP analysis shows marketing agencies willing to pay $500-1200/month for AIR, validating freemium-to-premium model.
terms.usageContext: Conducted during Problem Validation phase
terms.businessMeaning: Validates that customers will pay enough to support profitable business model
terms.technicalMeaning: Research deliverable with price sensitivity curves and value perception mapping
terms.constraints: WTP analysis MUST cite Monetizing Innovation methodology (Rule DR-002)
terms.citation: Monetizing Innovation: How Smart Companies Design the Product Around the Price by Madhavan Ramanujam and Georg Tacke
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents analyze WTP data to recommend pricing tiers and feature bundling strategies

terms.name: Latent Demand
terms.description: Customer need that exists but customers may not be fully aware of the problem or why solving it matters most. Requires education not just about the problem but why it matters.
terms.usageExample: Latent demand for AI-powered content optimization: marketers don't know how much time they waste on manual optimization until shown data.
terms.usageContext: Identified during Customer Discovery and Problem Validation
terms.businessMeaning: Opportunities where customer education can unlock new market demand
terms.technicalMeaning: Problem classification requiring educational content and awareness campaigns
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents identify latent demand patterns from interview data and suggest education strategies

terms.name: Customer Discovery (Phase 1)
terms.description: First phase of PMF process: systematic identification and analysis of target customer segments using structured heuristic framework. Duration: [x weeks].
terms.usageExample: BAIV conducted customer discovery with 25 marketing agencies (targetCustomers) identifying content optimization as sharpest problem.
terms.usageContext: First phase of PMF process; prerequisite for all subsequent phases
terms.businessMeaning: Understanding who customers are, what problems they have, and why those problems matter
terms.technicalMeaning: PMF process phase with orgId-mapped deliverables and validation rules
terms.constraints: MUST complete before Problem Validation (Rule PR-001); Requires >=n validated interviews (Rule DR-001)
terms.relationships: Produces ICP, Journey Maps, Sharpest Problems Matrix; precedes Problem Validation
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents analyze interview transcripts to identify patterns and auto-generate problem hierarchies

terms.name: Problem Validation (Phase 2)
terms.description: Second phase: verification that identified problems are sharpest problems worth 3X-5X investment using WTP methodology. Duration: [y weeks].
terms.usageExample: Problem validation confirmed content optimization as critical problem for 78% of target agencies with WTP of $800/month average.
terms.usageContext: Second phase; follows Customer Discovery, precedes Solution Development
terms.businessMeaning: Confirming customers care enough about problems to pay for solutions
terms.technicalMeaning: Phase with WTP deliverables and p% critical rating threshold
terms.constraints: Requires Customer Discovery completion (Rule PR-001); WTP must cite Monetizing Innovation (Rule DR-002)
terms.relationships: Consumes Customer Discovery outputs; produces WTP analysis and 3X-5X gap assessment
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents correlate problem severity ratings with WTP data to predict revenue potential

terms.name: PMF Business Rules Framework
terms.description: Comprehensive set of business rules governing PMF process execution and validation. Categories: Organization Rules (OR-xxx), Strategy Rules (SR-xxx), Process Rules (PR-xxx), Data Rules (DR-xxx).
terms.usageExample: Business Rule OR-001: 'Platform Owner MUST be identified before PMF process instantiation' enforced at process creation.
terms.usageContext: Enforced throughout PMF process at phase gates and data validation points
terms.businessMeaning: Quality gates ensuring PMF process follows best practices and maintains data integrity
terms.technicalMeaning: Programmatically enforced rules with severity levels (ERROR, WARNING, INFO)
terms.constraints: Rules with ERROR severity MUST be satisfied for phase progression
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents validate rule compliance and provide remediation suggestions for violations

terms.name: PMF Dependency Framework
terms.description: Defines all dependencies between PMF ontology and external ontologies: org:Organization, okr:OKR, vsom:VSOM, oaa:Registry.
terms.usageExample: PMF ontology depends on org:Organization for orgId foreign keys; relationship type: foreignKey, constraint: REQUIRED.
terms.usageContext: Defines external ontology requirements for PMF ontology operation
terms.businessMeaning: External systems and data required for PMF process to function
terms.technicalMeaning: Foreign key relationships and referential integrity constraints across ontologies
terms.constraints: All REQUIRED dependencies MUST be available; orgId MUST exist in org:Organization
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents check dependency availability and data freshness before PMF process execution

terms.name: OAA Registry Entry
terms.description: Metadata entry in OAA Ontology Schema Registry. Contains version, status, quality metrics, dependencies, and governance information.
terms.usageExample: PMF ontology registry entry: baiv:ontology:pmf-process-v2, status: active, schema.org alignment: 85%.
terms.usageContext: Created when ontology registered in OAA system; updated with each version
terms.businessMeaning: Official record of ontology existence, ownership, and usage
terms.technicalMeaning: Registry metadata with quality metrics and dependency tracking
terms.constraints: ontologyId MUST be unique; version MUST follow semantic versioning
terms.relationships: References dependent ontologies; tracks consumers and maintainers
terms.aiAgentUsage: AI agents query registry to discover ontologies and validate compatibility

usageGuidelines.forBusinessUsers: Focus on business meaning and usage context; use examples to understand application
usageGuidelines.forDevelopers: Focus on technical meaning, constraints, and relationships; implement validation rules
usageGuidelines.forDataScientists: Focus on metrics, AI agent usage, and measurement criteria
usageGuidelines.forProductManagers: Focus on process terms, frameworks, and strategic concepts
